title: Number Properties
learning_objectives: Apply even/odd and sign rules to predict the result of an expression.
Use divisibility rules (2, 3, 5, 6, 9, 10) and the idea that divisibility by a composite means divisibility by its prime parts.
Prime-factorize an integer and use it to count factors and find GCD/LCM.
Translate 'must be true' number-property statements into fast tests.

pedagogical_model.sequence: I do → we do → you do

opening.builds_on: Whole-number multiplication/division facts and place value.

do_now.instructions: No notes, no calculator. Answer from memory in 4 minutes.

items.prompt: Is 51 prime? Show why in one product.
What must be true for a number to be divisible by 6?

retrieval_starter.purpose: Surfaces factors, primes, even/odd, and the key idea that divisibility by a composite equals divisibility by its prime parts — exactly the tools today extends to factor-counting and GCD/LCM.
retrieval_starter.if_students_struggle: If factors of 24 are shaky, rebuild with a factor-pair ladder (1×24, 2×12, 3×8, 4×6) before moving on; today's factor counting depends on it.

questions.prompt: In 3,204, what is the value of the digit 2?

opening.prior_knowledge_bridge: You just found the factors of 24 by trial. That works for small numbers, but the GMAT asks about numbers like 72 — or 2³ × 3² × 5 — where listing is far too slow. Today we replace listing with prime factorization, the 'DNA' of an integer, which lets you count factors and find GCD/LCM in seconds.
opening.learning_intention: By the end you can prime-factorize an integer and use it to count its factors, find GCD/LCM, and settle 'must be true' even/odd and divisibility questions without listing.
success_criteria: I can write any integer up to ~200 as a product of primes.
I can count an integer's positive factors from its prime factorization.
I can predict the even/odd and the sign of an expression and justify it.

opening_script.move: Do Now on screen; learners answer Q1–5 from memory, silent, no notes.
Cold-call answers; spotlight Q5 — 'divisible by 6' = divisible by 2 AND 3.
Bridge: listing factors of 72 is slow; introduce prime factorization as the shortcut.
State learning intention + success criteria on the board.
Move to I do: count the factors of 72 by reasoning aloud.

i_do.stem: How many distinct positive factors does 72 have?
i_do.explanation: 72 = 2³ × 3². The number of positive factors is (3+1)(2+1) = 4 × 3 = 12.
think_aloud_steps: Any factor uses 0–3 twos (4 choices) and 0–2 threes (3 choices).
i_do.key_takeaway: Number of positive factors = product of (each prime's exponent + 1).

we_do.stem: If a is odd and b is even, which of the following must be ODD?
we_do.explanation: a is odd and b is even, so a + b = odd + even = odd. ab and 2a have an even factor (even); b − 2 is even − even = even; a + b + 1 flips a + b to even. Only a + b is odd.
scaffold_hints: Parity rules: even ± even = even; odd ± even = odd.
Anything multiplied by an even number is even.
Test with a = 3, b = 2 and check each option's parity.

immediate_feedback.if_correct: Right — odd + even is always odd; the ×even and +even options are forced even.
immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: Re-check parity: B and E have an even factor (even); C subtracts an even number (stays even); D adds 1 to an odd, making it even.

we_do.stem: What is the greatest common divisor (GCD) of 48 and 60?
we_do.explanation: 48 = 2⁴ × 3 and 60 = 2² × 3 × 5. The GCD takes each SHARED prime at its LOWEST power: 2² × 3 = 12.
scaffold_hints: GCD uses only the primes they share, each at its smaller exponent.

immediate_feedback.if_correct: Yes — 2² × 3 = 12 divides both, and nothing larger does.
immediate_feedback.if_incorrect: Don't multiply every prime. 5 appears only in 60, so it can't be in the GCD; use shared primes at their lowest exponent.

you_do.stem: If the product of two integers is odd, which of the following MUST be true?

options.E: At least one integer is even

you_do.explanation: A product is odd only when every factor is odd; a single even factor would make the product even. So both integers must be odd. Every other option requires or implies an even factor.

you_do.stem: Which of the following is a multiple of BOTH 4 and 6?
you_do.explanation: A number divisible by both 4 and 6 must be divisible by their LCM, 12. Only 36 = 12 × 3 qualifies (18, 28, 42, 54 each fail divisibility by 4 or by 6).

you_do.stem: If n = 2³ × 3² × 5, how many distinct positive factors does n have?
you_do.explanation: Factor count = product of (exponent + 1) over all primes: (3+1)(2+1)(1+1) = 4 × 3 × 2 = 24.

you_do.stem: If p and q are distinct prime numbers, each greater than 2, which of the following MUST be even?
you_do.explanation: Primes greater than 2 are odd. Odd + odd = even, so p + q is even. pq is odd; pq + 2 stays odd; 2p + 1 is odd; p + q + 1 is even + 1 = odd. Only p + q must be even.

sample_held_out_items.stem: What is the least common multiple (LCM) of 9 and 12?
sample_held_out_items.explanation: 9 = 3² and 12 = 2² × 3. The LCM takes each prime at its HIGHEST power: 2² × 3² = 36.

sample_held_out_items.stem: How many distinct prime factors does 84 have?
sample_held_out_items.explanation: 84 = 2² × 3 × 7. The distinct primes are 2, 3, and 7 — three of them (count distinct primes, not total factors).

mastery_check.definition: A topic is mastered at >=85% correct on new/held-out-style questions across >=2 spaced sessions with delay (PRD Section 9.2). Flashcard recall alone is not mastery; application under mixed, delayed conditions is the bar.
conditions: >=85% correct on new/held-out-style items (not flashcard recall) per session
sustained across >=2 spaced sessions with a delay between them
per-topic memory surfaced only after >=20 graded reviews (PRD give-up rule)
per-topic performance surfaced only after >=8 application attempts (PRD give-up rule)
mastery_check.on_fail: Not yet: route every miss to the required error log (tag why: content gap / reasoning flaw / timing / careless / trap), schedule spaced re-practice via topic-aware scheduling, then re-enter the we-do -> you-do loop before re-testing.
